disease.name: Chronic Skin Ulcer
disease.note: Full-thickness skin defect failing to heal. Venous, arterial, diabetic, or pressure etiology. Requires multidisciplinary wound care. In Kenya, chronic leg ulcers are commonly misattributed to 'spirits' or curses, leading to prolonged traditional treatment before medical presentation. Always perform an ABI to distinguish arterial from venous disease before applying compression — a mistake here can be catastrophic.
presentingComplaints: Non-healing wound on the leg for weeks
Painful open sore on foot or ankle
Dark discoloration around a leg wound
Previous leg ulcer that healed and recurred

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Deep infection/osteomyelitis/ischemia -> SEVERE; superficial chronic -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: How long has this wound been present?
Did it start from a minor injury or spontaneously?
Do you have varicose veins or leg swelling?
Do you get leg pain when walking that goes away with rest?
Do you have diabetes?
Do you have numbness or tingling in your feet?
Is the wound painful? Does it wake you at night?
Have you had an ulcer before? How was it treated?
Do you smoke?
Do you have high blood pressure or high cholesterol?
Have you had any previous surgery on your legs or veins?
Have you used any traditional medicines or herbs on this wound?
How is your general health and appetite?
Do you sit with your legs down for long periods?
Have you ever had a wound infection that required antibiotics?
Have you noticed any discharge or odor from the wound?
How long has this ulcer been present? Has it healed and recurred?
What started the ulcer: trauma, blister, insect bite, or spontaneous?
Describe the pain: worse at night? Worse with leg elevation? Relieved by hanging leg down?
Do you have varicose veins or a history of DVT?
Do you have diabetes? How well is it controlled?
Do you have high blood pressure, high cholesterol, or smoking history?
Do you have known peripheral arterial disease or previous leg bypass/stent?
Have you had any previous leg ulcers and how were they treated?
Do you have leg swelling (edema) that is worse at end of day?
Have you had any surgery or radiation to the affected leg?
Do you have numbness, tingling, or burning in your feet?
Do you have any systemic symptoms: fever, weight loss, night sweats?
Have you had any trauma to the leg, even a minor bump or scratch?
Do you have sickle cell disease or trait?
Do you have inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis)?
Do you have rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, or other autoimmune disease?
Have you had a wound culture or biopsy of this ulcer before?
What treatments have you tried (dressings, antibiotics, compression, traditional)?
For diabetics: Do you check your feet daily? Do you wear appropriate footwear?
What is your occupation? Are you on your feet all day?

examination_findings.finding: Full-thickness skin defect with well-defined borders
Wound location: gaiter area (venous), toes/heel (arterial), plantar (diabetic)
Venous signs: hemosiderin pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis, varicose veins
Arterial signs: hairless leg, shiny skin, thick nails, absent pulses
Neuropathic signs: loss of sensation on monofilament testing
Wound bed: slough, necrotic tissue, or healthy granulation
Periwound maceration or callus
Signs of infection: purulent discharge, erythema, warmth
ABI (Ankle-Brachial Index) <0.9 or >1.3
Edema: pitting or non-pitting
Probe-to-bone test for osteomyelitis
Lymphadenopathy in groin
Fever if infected
Document precise ulcer location: gaiter area (venous), foot/toes (arterial/diabetic), metatarsal heads (neuropathic)
Measure ulcer dimensions: length, width, and depth in cm
Describe ulcer borders: sloping (venous), punched-out (arterial), undermined (neuropathic)
Assess wound bed color and percentage of granulation, slough, eschar
Assess exudate type, amount, and odor
Inspect periwound skin for hemosiderin staining, lipodermatosclerosis, atrophie blanche
Palpate pedal pulses: dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial
Measure ankle-brachial pressure index (ABI) using Doppler
Assess leg edema: pitting vs non-pitting, extent, unilateral vs bilateral
Perform monofilament test (10-g Semmes-Weinstein) on the foot
Assess sensation: pinprick, light touch, vibration (128 Hz), proprioception
Inspect for varicose veins and perform cough impulse test
Look for signs of DVT: unilateral swelling, warmth, erythema, Homan sign
Palpate for inguinal and popliteal lymphadenopathy
Examine foot for deformities: Charcot, hammer toes, claw toes, hallux valgus
Inspect between toes and web spaces for fungal infection or fissures
Assess capillary refill time in toes (normal <3 seconds)
Perform Buerger test: elevation pallor and dependent rubor
Perform probe-to-bone test with sterile metal probe for osteomyelitis
Assess for signs of systemic disease: clubbing, cyanosis, Janeway lesions

bedside_scores.name: ABI (Ankle-Brachial Index)
components: Systolic BP at ankle / Systolic BP at arm

bedside_scores.name: Wagner Ulcer Grade (Diabetic Foot)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Higher grade = higher amputation risk. Grade 3+ requires multidisciplinary approach.

subtypes.name: Venous Ulcer (Gaiter Area)
subtypes.management: Compression therapy (multi-layer bandaging). Leg elevation. Wound debridement and dressings. Endovenous ablation of incompetent veins. Skin grafting for large ulcers.

subtypes.name: Arterial/Ischemic Ulcer
subtypes.management: Revascularization (endovascular or bypass). No compression. Wound care. Analgesia. Amputation if unsalvageable.

subtypes.name: Diabetic (Neuropathic) Ulcer
subtypes.management: Offloading (total contact cast). Sharp debridement of callus. Infection control (antibiotics). Revascularization if PAD. Blood glucose control. Multidisciplinary foot team.

subtypes.name: Mixed Etiology Ulcer (Venous + Arterial)
subtypes.management: Address arterial component first (revascularization if ABI <0.5 or severe symptoms). Modified compression (reduced 15-20 mmHg). Multidisciplinary approach.

subtypes.name: Sickle Cell Ulcer
subtypes.management: Wound care with compression if no arterial disease. Hydroxyurea. Blood transfusion for severe anemia. Pain management. Skin grafting. Multidisciplinary hematology-wound care approach.

management.protocolReference: European Wound Management Association Guidelines; IWGDF Diabetic Foot Guidelines

resuscitation: If infected/septic: IV fluids, blood cultures, IV antibiotics
analgesia: Morphine for severe arterial ulcer pain
antibiotics: Cover gram-positive and gram-negative: Co-amoxiclav or Clindamycin + Ciprofloxacin

venous.step5: Endovenous ablation or surgery for incompetent veins

diabetic.step1: Total contact cast or removable offloading device
diabetic.step2: Sharp debridement of all callus and necrotic tissue

general: Vascular risk factor management (statins, antiplatelets, smoking cessation)

pathways.criteria: gaiter area, shallow, present pulses, venous disease
pathways.treatment: Multi-layer compression bandaging. Leg elevation. Wound debridement. Dressings (hydrocolloid, foam). Endovenous ablation of incompetent veins.

pathways.criteria: distal, absent pulses, low ABI, neuropathy
pathways.treatment: Vascular assessment. Revascularization. Offloading (total contact cast). Debridement. Infection control. Blood glucose control. Wound care MDT.

operative_templates.procedure: Split Skin Grafting for Chronic Venous Ulcer
operative_templates.indication: Large venous ulcer with clean granulating bed
steps: Assess wound bed (must be clean, granulating, no slough)
Harvest split-thickness skin graft (0.012-0.015 inch) from anterior thigh
Dress donor site with paraffin gauze

watchFor: chronic non-healing with rolled everted edges

dischargeCriteria: Appropriate offloading or compression in place
Follow-up with wound care team arranged

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: A chronic leg ulcer is a wound that has not healed for more than 4 weeks. It is usually caused by problems with blood circulation — either veins not returning blood to the heart properly (venous), arteries not bringing enough blood to the leg (arterial), or nerve damage from diabetes (neuropathic). The treatment depends on the cause.
patient_education.warningSigns: Wound getting larger, increasing pain, fever, foul-smelling discharge, blackening of the wound edges, severe pain at rest.
patient_education.activity: For venous ulcers: elevate legs when sitting, avoid prolonged standing. For arterial ulcers: rest, keep legs warm but avoid direct heat. Walk gently as tolerated.
patient_education.diet: High protein diet for wound healing. Control blood sugar if diabetic. Stay well hydrated.
patient_education.medication: Take all medications as prescribed for your underlying condition (diabetes, hypertension, cholesterol). Complete antibiotic course if prescribed.
patient_education.followUp: Regular wound care follow-up. Weekly initially. Report any deterioration immediately.

before_discharge: Wound care training for patient and family

after_discharge.action: Wound care review, dressing change assessment

when_to_return: Difficulty with wound care at home

pathophysiologyDetail: A chronic skin ulcer is a full-thickness skin defect that fails to progress through the normal phases of wound healing (hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, remodeling) within 4 weeks. The pathophysiology is determined by the underlying etiology, which must be identified before appropriate treatment can be initiated. Venous ulcers (50-60% of chronic leg ulcers) result from chronic venous hypertension due to valvular incompetence (primary varicose veins) or post-thrombotic venous damage. Venous hypertension leads to extravasation of fibrinogen and red blood cells into the perivascular space, forming a fibrin cuff around dermal capillaries that impairs oxygen and nutrient diffusion. Hemosiderin deposition from degraded red blood cells causes the characteristic brownish skin discoloration (hemosiderin staining) and lipodermatosclerosis (fibrosis and induration of the subcutaneous tissue). The final common pathway is a cascade of chronic inflammation, tissue hypoxia, and failed re-epithelialization. Venous ulcers are typically located in the 'gaiter area' (lower third of the leg, between the ankle and mid-calf), are shallow with irregular borders, and are associated with varicose veins, leg edema, and skin changes (stasis dermatitis, lipodermatosclerosis, atrophie blanche).

Arterial ulcers (15-25%) result from peripheral arterial disease (PAD) causing chronic tissue hypoperfusion. Atherosclerosis of the lower limb arteries (most commonly the superficial femoral artery) leads to reduced ankle-brachial pressure index (ABI <0.9). These ulcers are typically located on the distal foot (toes, heel, lateral malleolus), are deep with a 'punched-out' appearance, pale or necrotic wound bed, and are exquisitely painful (pain is worse at night and with elevation, relieved by dependency). Diabetic foot ulcers (15-25%) are neuropathic in origin: peripheral sensory neuropathy from chronic hyperglycemia leads to repetitive trauma on a insensate foot, typically over pressure points (metatarsal heads, plantar hallux, heel). The ulcer is painless (or minimally painful despite extensive depth), deep, often infected, and associated with peripheral neuropathy (loss of light touch, pinprick, and vibration sense on monofilament testing) and peripheral vascular disease (which may coexist). The key distinguishing feature: a painless ulcer on the plantar surface of the foot in a diabetic patient is neuropathic until proven otherwise.

In Kenya, chronic skin ulcers present with several distinctive features. Lymphatic filariasis (Wuchereria bancrofti) is endemic in coastal regions and causes lymphedema that can progress to elephantiasis with chronic non-healing ulcers of the lower extremity. Buruli ulcer (Mycobacterium ulcerans infection) is present in parts of West and Central Africa but has been reported in East Africa as well, presenting as a painless, slowly progressive ulcer with undermined edges and necrotic subcutaneous tissue. Kaposi sarcoma (both HIV-associated and endemic forms) can present as chronic ulcerated nodules on the lower extremities. The high prevalence of diabetes (estimated 5-10% in urban Kenyan adults) and peripheral arterial disease (especially among smokers and hypertensive patients) means that diabetic and arterial ulcers are common. HIV infection, with its associated immunosuppression, impairs wound healing at every stage — decreased inflammatory response, reduced angiogenesis, impaired collagen synthesis, and increased susceptibility to wound infection. The misdiagnosis of chronic leg ulcers as 'spiritual' or 'cursed' leads to prolonged traditional treatment (herbal dressings, scarification) before medical presentation, by which time ulcers are frequently large, infected, and have already caused irreversible damage to underlying structures.

landmarkTrials.trial: EVRA Trial (Early Venous Reflux Ablation vs Deferred for Venous Ulcers)
landmarkTrials.population: 450 patients with venous leg ulcers across 20 UK vascular centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early endovenous ablation (laser, radiofrequency, or foam sclerotherapy) within 2 weeks vs deferred ablation after ulcer healing
landmarkTrials.outcome: 24-week healing rate: 85% early vs 76% deferred (p=0.01); faster healing (hazard ratio 1.38); lower 1-year recurrence (12% vs 28%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Changed practice: early venous intervention improves healing and reduces recurrence of venous ulcers
landmarkTrials.reference: Gohel MS, et al. A randomized trial of early endovenous ablation in venous ulceration (EVRA). N Engl J Med. 2018;378(22):2105-2114.

landmarkTrials.trial: Multilayer Compression Bandaging for Venous Ulcers (Cochrane Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 25 RCTs (3,300 patients) comparing compression systems
landmarkTrials.intervention: Multilayer compression (3-4 layers) vs single-layer or no compression
landmarkTrials.outcome: Multilayer compression healed more ulcers at 24 weeks (OR 2.0, 95% CI 1.5-2.7); better than single-layer
landmarkTrials.impact: Multilayer high-compression bandaging (30-40 mmHg at ankle, graduating to lower pressure at knee) is the standard of care for venous ulcers
landmarkTrials.reference: O'Meara S, et al. Compression for venous leg ulcers. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;11:CD000265.

landmarkTrials.trial: Negative Pressure Wound Therapy for Diabetic Foot Ulcers (DFU RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 335 patients with post-amputation diabetic foot wounds across 30 US centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: NPWT at -125 mmHg vs standard moist wound therapy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Faster time to wound closure (82 vs 102 days, p=0.04); higher proportion closed at 16 weeks (43% vs 29%)
landmarkTrials.impact: NPWT is effective for diabetic foot wounds but cost limits availability in Kenya
landmarkTrials.reference: Blume PA, et al. Comparison of negative pressure wound therapy versus standard wound treatment for diabetic foot ulcers. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(4):631-636.

landmarkTrials.trial: INTACT (Topical Oxygen Therapy for Chronic Wounds)
landmarkTrials.population: RCT of 245 patients with chronic diabetic foot ulcers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Topical continuous diffusion of oxygen (TCO2) for 24h/day vs standard care
landmarkTrials.outcome: Higher complete closure rate at 12 weeks (41% vs 22%, p=0.003)
landmarkTrials.impact: Novel therapy; not widely available in Kenya but shows promise for refractory ulcers
landmarkTrials.reference: Niederauer MQ, et al. Continuous diffusion of oxygen for chronic diabetic foot ulcers. Wound Repair Regen. 2020;28(5):639-647.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: CLASSIFY ULCER -> ASSESS VASCULAR STATUS -> IDENTIFY ETIOLOGY -> TREAT -> PREVENT RECURRENCE. CLASSIFY: The first step is to determine the ulcer type based on location, appearance, and associated findings. Venous ulcer: gaiter area (lower third of leg), shallow, irregular borders, hemosiderin staining, lipodermatosclerosis, varicose veins, edema, pain improved with elevation. Arterial ulcer: foot/toes/heel, deep 'punched-out,' pale/necrotic base, no granulation, painful (worse at night, with elevation), hair loss on foot, shiny skin, cool to touch, reduced pulses. Diabetic neuropathic ulcer: plantar surface (metatarsal heads, hallux), deep, often painless, surrounding callus, peripheral neuropathy (loss of monofilament sensation, absent ankle reflexes), warm foot with palpable pulses (unless PAD coexists). Pressure ulcer: over bony prominence (sacrum, heel, trochanter), history of immobility. Atypical ulcer (biopsy indicated): irregular margins, rolled edges, violaceous color, location not typical, no response to standard treatment for 4-6 weeks, suspected malignancy (squamous cell carcinoma in chronic ulcers), vasculitis, or infection (TB, fungal, Buruli). ASSESS VASCULAR STATUS: Perform ankle-brachial pressure index (ABI) measurement on ALL patients with leg ulcers BEFORE applying compression. Normal ABI: 0.9-1.3. Venous disease: ABI >0.9 (normal arterial flow) AND venous reflux on duplex ultrasound. Arterial disease: ABI <0.9 (mild-moderate: 0.5-0.9; severe: <0.5). Diabetic patients may have falsely elevated ABI >1.3 due to medial arterial calcification (Moenckeberg sclerosis) — in these cases, toe-brachial index (TBI >0.7 normal) or transcutaneous oxygen pressure (TcPO2) is more reliable. IDENTIFY ETIOLOGY: Venous duplex ultrasound (reflux in saphenous or perforator veins) confirms venous etiology. Arterial duplex with segmental pressures and waveforms localizes arterial disease. Neuropathy assessment: 10-g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament test (inability to feel the monofilament at 3 of 10 sites indicates loss of protective sensation), biothesiometry (vibration perception threshold), and ankle reflex assessment. Wound swab culture (for infection) and wound biopsy (for suspected malignancy, vasculitis, or atypical infection). TREAT: Venous ulcer: high-compression multilayer bandaging (30-40 mmHg at ankle) + early venous ablation. Arterial ulcer: revascularization (angioplasty or bypass) if ABI <0.5 or disabling claudication; then wound care. Diabetic ulcer: offloading (total contact cast, removable cast walker, or therapeutic footwear), sharp debridement, infection control (antibiotics if cellulitis or osteomyelitis), revascularization if PAD present. For all ulcers: moist wound healing (foam, alginate, hydrocolloid, or hydrogel dressings based on wound bed and exudate), infection control (topical antimicrobials: silver, iodine, honey for mild infection; systemic antibiotics for spreading cellulitis), debridement (sharp, autolytic, enzymatic, or larval), and adjunctive therapies (NPWT for deep ulcers, skin grafting for large epithelialization defects). PREVENT RECURRENCE: Venous: lifelong compression stockings (23-32 mmHg), leg elevation when resting, weight management, exercise (walking). Arterial: smoking cessation, antiplatelet therapy, statin, blood pressure control, supervised exercise program. Diabetic: daily foot inspection, well-fitting therapeutic footwear, regular podiatry, glycemic control (HbA1c target <7.0%), annual foot examination. In the Kenyan context, honey (medical-grade Manuka honey or locally sourced, gamma-irradiated honey) is a highly effective, low-cost topical antimicrobial that promotes wound healing and is widely available across the country.

managementPearls.pearl: NEVER apply compression bandaging to a leg ulcer without first checking the ABI (ankle-brachial index). If ABI <0.5, compression will worsen ischemia and may cause tissue necrosis and limb loss. In Kenyan settings where Doppler ABI measurement is not available, a simple rule: a palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse in a patient without rest pain or gangrene is a reasonable surrogate for ABI >0.5.

Diabetic foot ulcers require aggressive offloading. The gold standard is a total contact cast (TCC), which reduces pressure at the ulcer site by 80-90%. In Kenya, TCC application requires specialized training but achieves healing rates of 90% at 12 weeks vs 40% with standard dressings. A removable cast walker (RCW) is an acceptable alternative if the patient commits to wearing it at all times.

Medical-grade honey (Manuka or locally sterilized honey) is an excellent, low-cost topical antimicrobial for chronic ulcers. It creates a moist wound environment, has bactericidal activity against MRSA and ESBL organisms, debrides necrotic tissue through autolysis, and promotes granulation. Cost in Kenya: approximately KES 500-1,500 per 100g jar vs KES 3,000-5,000 for silver-based dressings.

A chronic ulcer that fails to improve after 4-6 weeks of standard treatment requires re-evaluation of the diagnosis. Biopsy the wound edge (including a portion of the ulcer edge and the adjacent normal skin) to exclude: squamous cell carcinoma (Marjolin ulcer in chronic wounds), vasculitis, pyoderma gangrenosum, or atypical infection (TB, fungal, Buruli ulcer). In Kenya, a high index of suspicion for Kaposi sarcoma (HIV-associated) and Buruli ulcer (if history of wetland exposure) is warranted.

Venous leg ulcers: the combination of multilayer high-compression bandaging (changed weekly) with early referral for endovenous ablation of refluxing saphenous veins achieves the highest healing rates and lowest recurrence. In Kenya, where endovenous ablation is rarely available, compression therapy alone, combined with leg elevation and exercise, remains the mainstay. Ultrasound-guided foam sclerotherapy (using 1-3% sodium tetradecyl sulfate) is a low-cost alternative to endovenous ablation that can be performed in an outpatient setting.

operativeNuances.nuance: Split-thickness skin grafting (STSG) is the most common surgical procedure for chronic leg ulcers that have a clean, granulating wound bed and are ready for epithelial coverage. The wound bed must be free of infection, with healthy red granulation tissue covering >95% of the ulcer. Apply the graft (0.012-0.015 inch thickness, meshed 1:1.5 for leg ulcers to allow drainage) to the wound bed, secure with staples or sutures, and apply a bolster dressing or NPWT over the graft for 5 days. Meshed grafts are preferred over sheet grafts as they allow serous drainage and conform better to irregular wound surfaces. Contraindications: active infection, exposed bone without periosteum (requires flap coverage), and untreated arterial insufficiency.

For deep diabetic foot ulcers with exposed bone, the concern for osteomyelitis requires a bone biopsy or probe-to-bone test (sterile metal probe inserted into the ulcer; if hard, gritty bone is felt, osteomyelitis is present with 85% sensitivity). If osteomyelitis is confirmed, treatment requires: (1) surgical debridement of infected bone (partial calcanectomy, ray amputation, or transmetatarsal amputation depending on the extent), (2) 4-6 weeks of culture-directed IV antibiotics, and (3) delayed closure once infection is cleared. Diabetic foot osteomyelitis is NOT treated with antibiotics alone — surgical resection of infected bone is usually required.

prognosis.venousUlcer_healingRate: 60-70% heal within 6 months with compression; 80-90% within 12 months; 10-15% remain unhealed at 2 years
prognosis.arterialUlcer_healingRate: 50-60% heal within 6 months after revascularization; 20-30% without revascularization
prognosis.diabeticUlcer_healingRate: 70-80% heal within 6 months with optimal care (offloading, debridement, infection control, revascularization); 15-25% progress to amputation
prognosis.recurrenceRate_venous: 30-70% at 1 year without compression; 15-30% with compression stockings; 12% after EVRA (early ablation)
prognosis.amputationRisk_diabetic: 15-25% of diabetic foot ulcers ultimately require lower extremity amputation; diabetes accounts for >70% of non-traumatic amputations worldwide
prognosis.kenyanConsiderations: Higher rates of mixed-etiology ulcers, delayed presentation with large infected ulcers, limited access to vascular intervention and specialized wound care, high prevalence of HIV and TB as comorbid factors impairing healing
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Correct identification of ulcer etiology, adequate arterial perfusion (ABI >0.5), infection control, nutritional status, and patient adherence to offloading and compression regimens
